Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Optative blessing formula; verb + possessed noun phrase, with masculine/feminine second-person suffix alternation.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A warm thank-you blessing wishing that the addressee’s household remains full, living, and healthy.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Tawdi sagi; Aloho nšor l-baytux b-ḥayye.
Usage Contexts: thanks, hospitality, family visits, church service, neighborly help, holidays
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Turabdin house culture, where a full household signals health, continuity, and communal dignity.
First Example Sentence: Bëṯër me d-Saro mʿadrola l-emi bi čaye, emi mërlala: “Comër baytax.”
Second Example Sentence: U qašo šqille i trumo men Yuhanon w mërlēle: “Comër baytux, ḥawro.”
Third Example Sentence: Qam ḥašo du saṯwo, i ganino mlel-le bayto w kulun mërwale: “Comër baytux.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed optative clause; verb + definite noun phrase functioning as a farewell blessing.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A holiday or visit-closing farewell wishing life, people, and blessing to remain in the home.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Fušu bë šlomo; Aloho nšor l-bayto b-ṭobo.
Usage Contexts: farewells, feast days, house visits, elder speech, family gatherings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Associated with Syriac/Turoyo domestic hospitality and feast-day leave-taking in Turabdin and diaspora homes.
First Example Sentence: Bë yoma d-Edo, qaḏam d-nāfqu, jëdde mërlan: “Comër u bayto.”
Second Example Sentence: Lawla mërwal “fušu bë šlomo,” bas “comër u bayto” heno m-basimuṯo.
Third Example Sentence: Qam d-nzalin l-Mëḏyaḏ, xalto mḥadralan w mërlan: “Comër u bayto, yalune.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed prepositional phrase; preposition + plural body-part noun, used as a discourse response.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Gladly, with honor, or willingly; the speaker accepts a request with respect.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: B-ḥḏo gëd oṯeno; maṣyono w roḥamno d-mʿadranux.
Usage Contexts: requests, favors, workplace help, family duties, politeness formulas
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A Semitic honor formula based on the eye as a site of respect and readiness; common in regional politeness.
First Example Sentence: “Tayto qam šëmšo,” mërlan, w Saro jawbala: “Cal ac cayne.”
Second Example Sentence: I malpanito bʿila d-nqro i risālo, w ono mërli: “Cal ac cayne, moro.”
Third Example Sentence: U šabbo qaryale l-ḥawre d-mʿadriwle, w hane mërle: “Cal ac cayne, qurban.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative imperative-like politeness formula; negator + verb + prepositional object with first-person possessive suffix.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Forgive me; do not hold my mistake against me.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sliḥli; ono ġalṭone w baci d-msadqeno.
Usage Contexts: apology, hospitality, elder address, clerical address, workplace correction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects a regional etiquette of acknowledging one’s fault before elders, guests, clergy, or respected community members.
First Example Sentence: “Lo ḥayrët lu qëṣurayḏi, tawin qliḏo b-bayto,” mërlēle.
Second Example Sentence: I šabre sëmle ḥoṣono b-daftar w mërlēle l-malpan: “Lo ḥayrët lu qëṣurayḏi.”
Third Example Sentence: Bë šuġlo, Saro mërla: “Lo ḥayrët lu qëṣurayḏi, i waʿdo gëd mḥadrano.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed first-person experiential clause; participial form + reflexive/possessive noun ‘self/soul’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: It slipped my mind; I forgot because I was distracted or flustered.
Usage Contexts: apologies, domestic tasks, school, scheduling, errands, storytelling
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses ruḥo ‘self/soul’ to frame memory failure as a momentary loss of inner attention rather than intentional neglect.
First Example Sentence: “Ṭacyono ruḥi, qam maṯyono u sefer,” mërla Meryam.
Second Example Sentence: Bë sugho, malka mšayele qimo, w u ṭalbo mërle: “Ṭacyono ruḥi.”
Third Example Sentence: Ḥar d-qam wacdo, Quryāqos hzerle l-pumo w mërle: “Ṭacyono ruḥi, ḥawri.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Existential negation; ‘there is not’ + concrete noun ‘knot/problem’, lexicalized as reassurance.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: There is no problem; the matter is manageable and should not worry anyone.
Usage Contexts: appointments, favors, technical support, daily planning, social reassurance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on the image of a knot as a complication; widely transparent in everyday problem-solving speech.
First Example Sentence: “I kerso daq motor lo ko-mšaqqlo?” “Layt qëṭro, gëd nsaqqlono qam caṣriye.”
Second Example Sentence: “Aḏyawma lo maṣyono d-oṯe,” mērle; Saro jawbala: “Layt qëṭro.”
Third Example Sentence: “I faylo xriwto yo,” mērle u mhandës; “layt qëṭro, kitlan backup.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Light-verb idiom; ‘make/do’ + noun, conjugated for subject and tense.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: He became curious, worried, or mentally preoccupied about something.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sagi tfakkarle; baci d-yodo mën yo.
Usage Contexts: storytelling, family news, uncertainty, investigation, gossip, emotional narration
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A flavorful domestic metaphor: mental stirring is framed through märaq ‘soup/stew’, echoing household imagery.
First Example Sentence: Aḥuni sëmle märaq čunke i ḫabrto lo mṭet-la bi waqt.
Second Example Sentence: Bëṯër me d-šmeʿle u qalo, u mori sëmle märaq w mšayele mën yo.
Third Example Sentence: I qaḏamto, ḥawroye sëmle märaq cal i paluto d-qarye.

Idiom (Turoyo): Fušu bë šlomo / fëš bë šlomo
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Imperative farewell formula; plural/singular imperative + prepositional phrase ‘in peace’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Goodbye; remain safe, settled, and at peace after I leave.
Usage Contexts: farewells, lessons, visits, phone calls, community gatherings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on šlomo ‘peace’, a central Syriac/Aramaic greeting and farewell concept with religious and communal resonance.
First Example Sentence: Bë lëlyo, babo mërle l-yale: “Fušu bë šlomo, bāker gëd nḥaze.”
Second Example Sentence: Qam d-tisacn l-bus, i malpanito mërla: “Fušu bë šlomo, la tdbalin.”
Third Example Sentence: “Fëš bë šlomo, Yuhanon,” mērle u ḥawro w šqille tarco.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: First-person plural movement/farewell formula + prepositional phrase ‘in peace’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Let us go in peace; a courteous departure formula used by the people leaving.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Aḥna gëd nāzlin; qam ḥazina ḥreto.
Usage Contexts: departures, guest-leaving, travel, weddings, evening visits
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Complements fëš/fušu bë šlomo by marking the departing group’s side of the farewell exchange.
First Example Sentence: Bëṯər qaḥwe, Šmuni qam nfaq w mërlan: “Zax bë šlomo.”
Second Example Sentence: U safrono mërle l-qarye: “Zax bë šlomo; gëd naḏcarno lkulxun.”
Third Example Sentence: Bë ḥedo d-mëštuṯo, i xalto mërla: “Zax bë šlomo w ḥzo Mëḏyaḏ b-ḥubo.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Blessing formula; divine subject + verb + prepositional object with gender/number suffix.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May God bless you; an expression of gratitude, approval, or goodwill after help or good news.
Usage Contexts: thanks, charity, family blessing, church, condolence support, elder speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Syriac Christian blessing practice and the role of religious language in everyday Turoyo politeness.
First Example Sentence: U qašo mērle l-faʿole: “Aloho mbarëx aclayye,” qam d-yazli l-paluto.
Second Example Sentence: “Aloho mbarëx aclux,” mērla emi l-ibro d-mʿadrola b-suġo.
Third Example Sentence: Bëṯər trumo, kulan mërlan: “Aloho mbarëx aclux, aw nošo d-ṭobo.”
